planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; calico plant produces its most vivid and complex foliage colors in bright light — in deep shade the colors fade significantly toward green.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant transplants in well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; calico plant (Joseph's coat alternanthera, party time plant) is a tropical South American annual grown entirely for its brilliantly colored, small foliage — the tiny, densely-packed leaves are produced in vivid combinations of red, orange, yellow, chartreuse, pink, bronze, and variously multicolored patterns depending on cultivar, creating a remarkably intense and permanent color effect that serves a similar role to coleus in partially shaded garden settings. It is used primarily as a low edging plant, knot garden element, and mosaic bedding plant where its compact, clipping-tolerant growth and intense color can be used to create formal patterns. In full sun the colors are most vivid; partial shade produces acceptable but somewhat more muted tones. Clipping to maintain compact shape is recommended. 'Red Threads' (fine-textured red), 'Yellow Fine Leaf', and 'Party Time' (pink and red) are popular selections. Frost-tender; treated as annual north of zone 9.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; stromanthe requires consistently high humidity — in dry conditions, leaf edges brown and the striking variegation fades. Misting or a humid microclimate is beneficial.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; stromanthe (Triostar, 'Tricolor') is a Brazilian tropical perennial of the prayer plant family (Marantaceae) that produces some of the most dramatically colored foliage of any shade tropical plant — the long, lance-shaped, leathery leaves are boldly painted in irregular splashes, streaks, and blotches of cream-white, pale green, and deep forest green on the upper surface, while the undersides are uniformly, intensely wine-red to magenta, creating a bicolor leaf display that is visible from both above and below. Like its relatives calathea and maranta, the leaves fold upward in the evening (a response called nyctinasty, hence 'prayer plant' family), revealing the red undersides in a daily display. Requires consistently moist soil and high humidity — the primary challenge of growing stromanthe successfully in hot, dry climates. Excellent in shaded tropical borders, outdoor living spaces, and bright-shade patio containers in zones 10–12. The cultivar 'Triostar' is the standard variegated selection widely grown.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; ctenanthe tolerates the deepest shade of the prayer plant family and maintains its leaf coloring in quite low light conditions where stromanthe and calathea would fade.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; never-never plant is a Brazilian tropical perennial of the prayer plant family (Marantaceae) that produces long, lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive, precisely patterned, feathered silvery-gray variegation on a rich dark-green background — the alternating dark and light bands create a herringbone or fish-bone pattern that has a formal, almost graphic quality unlike the more organic markings of calathea. The undersides of the leaves are deep burgundy-red to maroon. It is among the most shade-tolerant of the Marantaceae family, maintaining acceptable growth and variegation in quite dim interior spaces where other prayer plant relatives would decline. Like calathea and maranta, the leaves exhibit nyctinasty (night leaf movement), folding upward in the evening. More tolerant of irregular watering than calathea. 'Tricolor' (cream, white, and green irregular blotching) is the most widely grown cultivar selection.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade after frost; peacock plant is one of the most humidity-demanding of the Marantaceae — crispy leaf edges and fading leaf patterns are the primary symptoms of insufficient humidity.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; peacock plant (cathedral windows, cathedral plant) is a Brazilian tropical perennial of the prayer plant family (Marantaceae) considered by many to have the finest and most elaborate leaf patterning of any ornamental tropical plant — the oval leaves are covered with an intricate pattern of dark-green ovals and irregular markings on a silver-green background that exactly replicates the eye-spot pattern of a peacock's tail feather, with deeper-green undersurface markings visible through the semitranslucent leaf when backlit. The effect is almost stained-glass-like when the leaves are held up to light. Requires genuinely high ambient humidity (above 60%) for the leaves to maintain crisp edges and clear, sharp variegation — in dry conditions, the leaf edges turn brown and the pattern loses clarity. An outstanding houseplant for humid environments and a spectacular specimen for outdoor tropical shade gardens in zones 10–12. Sensitive to fluoride and chlorine in tap water; use distilled or rain water.

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens or divide root suckers in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; harlequin glorybower spreads by root suckers and should be given adequate space — the suckering habit can be managed by mowing around the perimeter or installing a root barrier.

directions: Plant container specimens or root suckers in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; harlequin glorybower is a large, bold, East Asian deciduous shrub or small tree that provides the most remarkable autumn display of any ornamental tree or shrub in its zone range — the large, dark-purple flower buds open in late summer into clusters of pure-white, intensely fragrant, jasmine-like tubular flowers with long protruding stamens, and as the petals fall, the persistent calyxes remain and flush deep rose-pink to magenta, while the seed inside each calyx ripens to a brilliant, glistening, turquoise-blue to deep cobalt-blue, creating a simultaneous display of vivid blue berries in inflated red calyxes that is completely unlike anything else in the temperate landscape. The large, oval leaves smell unpleasantly if crushed. It tolerates partial shade, spreads by root suckers into colonies (which can be managed by mowing), and blooms most freely in full sun. The blue berries attract and feed birds throughout winter. 'Carnival' (yellow-variegated leaves) is an ornamental selection.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
